ROUGE-L (Lin and Hovy, 2003) evaluates text similarity based on the longest common subsequence. Specifically, we reported F1 score.
METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) evaluates text similarity by using unigram matching between machine-generated outputs and human reference texts.
BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2019) computes the similarity between generated and reference texts using contextual word embeddings.
A3CU (Liu et al., 2023b) evaluates summarization quality by directly comparing texts without extracting atomic content units, providing a human-aligned assessment of content similarity.

TAPAS-Acc (Liu et al., 2022) is a reference-free metric that uses a TAPAS (Herzig et al., 2020) model fine-tuned on the TabFact Chen et al. (2019) dataset to assess the faithfulness of generated text by verifying factual consistency.

G-Eval (Liu et al., 2023a) assesses the quality of generated text based on specific evaluation criteria using LLMs. We adopt G-Eval to separately evaluate faithfulness and completeness on a 5-point Likert scale. The evaluation prompts are provided in Table 18.

To assess the effectiveness of multi-table retrieval, we evaluate both general-purpose retrievers and table-specific retrievers. General-purpose retrievers serve as strong baselines, given their widespread use in plain retrieval tasks. Table-specific retrievers are designed explicitly for tables, leveraging table-specific representations for improved retrieval performance. Specifically, we consider the following retrievers as the baselines:

• BM25 (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009) is a sparse retriever that relies on a traditional bag-of-words representation to score relevant documents. In this study, we rank linearized tables as documents based on term frequency and inverse document frequency, leveraging lexical overlaps between questions and tables.

• DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020) employs BERT-based encoders to independently map questions and documents into a shared embedding space. It learns dense vector representations that enable semantic similarity matching. In this study, we implement DPR utilizing facebook/dpr- encoder-multiset-base.

• Contriever (Izacard et al., 2021) encodes questions and documents into a shared embedding space, optimizing for relevance through contrastive learning. In this study, we implement Contriever utilizing facebook/ contriever-msmacro.

• DTR (Herzig et al., 2021) is a table-specific dense retriever built on a TAPAS (Herzig et al., 2020) backbone, designed to effectively encode tabular structures and relationships. In this study, we fine-tune google/tapas-base.

• TableLlama (Zhang et al., 2023) is Llama 2-7B (Touvron et al., 2023) based open-source LLM-based generalist model that is designed for a variety of table-based tasks. We utilize TableLlama to generate question and table embeddings for table retrieval task. In this study, we fine-tune osunlp/TableLlama.

To comprehensively compare the performance of insight generation across multiple tables, we consider three types of baselines: proprietary LLMs, open-source LLMs, and SOTA table question-answering methods. Proprietary LLMs encompass commercial models renowned for their advanced reasoning and high-quality text generation capabilities. Open-source LLMs, serving as transparent and adaptable alternatives, leverage large-scale training data and advanced architectures to deliver performance that is competitive with proprietary counterparts. SOTA TQA methods are specialized approaches explicitly engineered for complex, table-aware reasoning tasks, prioritizing accuracy in tabular question-answering. We consider the following models as the baseline generators:

• o3-mini (OpenAI, 2025b) is a smaller yet advanced LLM developed by OpenAI, which is designed to efficiently solve complex problems by breaking them into constituent parts. In this study, we leverage o3-mini-2025-01-31 with reasoning effort parameter as medium.

• GPT-4o (OpenAI, 2024) is an advanced OpenAI’s proprietary LLM known for its enhanced reasoning capabilities and performance across various disciplines. In this study, we leverage gpt-4o-2024-08-06 checkpoint.

• Claude 3.5 Sonnet (Anthropic, ) is developed by Anthropic. It features improvements in coding proficiency and multimodal capabilities. In this study, we leverage claude-3-5-sonnet -20241022 checkpoint.

GPT-4o (OpenAI, 2024) is an advanced OpenAI’s proprietary LLM known for its enhanced reasoning capabilities and performance across various disciplines. In this study, we leverage gpt-4o-2024-08-06 checkpoint.

Claude 3.5 Sonnet (Anthropic, ) is developed by Anthropic. It features improvements in coding proficiency and multimodal capabilities. In this study, we leverage claude-3-5-sonnet -20241022 checkpoint.

• DeepSeek-R1-8B (DeepSeek-AI, 2025) is distilled version of DeepSeek-R1, which is a open-source LLM released by DeepSeek AI. In this study, we utilize unsloth/DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama -8B, which is based on Llama-3.1-8B.
• Qwen2-7B (Yang et al., 2024) is an open-source LLM developed by Alibaba Cloud, with the largest model containing 72 billion parameters. In this study, we utilize Qwen/Qwen2-7B- Instruct.

• Gemma-7B (Mesnard et al., 2024) is an open-source LLM developed by Google DeepMind, which is known for its multilingual capabilities and creative outputs. In this study, we utilize google/gemma-7b-it.

• Llama 3.1-8B (MetaAI, 2024) is an open-source LLM released by Meta AI, which is available in multiple sizes up to 405 billion parameters. In this study, we utilize meta-llama/Llama- 3.1-8B-Instruct.
• Mistral-7B (Jiang et al., 2023) is an open-source LLM developed by Mistral AI. In this study, we utilize mistralai/Mistral-7B- Instruct-v0.3.

• Chain-of-Table (Wang et al., 2024b) is LLM-based method designed to enhance table-based reasoning. It employs iterative reconstruction of input table through dynamic tabular operations.

• TaPERA (Zhao et al., 2024) a modular framework designed to enhance faithfulness and interpretability in long-form table question answering by combining a QA-based content planner and execution-based reasoning..
• Dater (Ye et al., 2023) focuses on selecting relevant information from input tables and providing contextual information to support the statement verification process.
Chain-of-Table (Wang et al., 2024b) is LLM-based method designed to enhance table-based reasoning. It employs iterative reconstruction of input table through dynamic tabular operations.

Following recent studies that utilize language models for table-related tasks (Chen, 2023; Seo et al., 2024), we serialize the table input into a flattened sequence to effectively represent table data for language model processing. The table title is enclosed within [TITLE] tags, followed by the table headers marked with [HEADER], where individual column names are separated by a vertical bar (|). Each row is prefixed with a [ROW] tag and an index, while cell values are separated by a vertical bar. This approach ensures that the table format is preserved while making the input compatible with language models. For example, the input table is formatted as follows:
[TITLE] title [HEADER] col 1 | col2 | ... [ROW 1] cell 1,1 | cell 1,2 | ... [ROW 2] cell 2,1 | ...

For the table-specific retriever, we fine-tune each model on a single-table QA dataset using the AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of for 5 epochs. To efficiently finetune the model, we adopt LoRA and set the parameters as rr = 8, α\alpha = 32. We use a constant learning rate schedule set at 2e-5, and train with the batch size of 1 on 4 NVIDIA A100 GPU. The inference of open-source LLMs are conducted using vLLM framework (Kwon et al., 2023). We set temperature to 0.0 for efficient and robust output generation.

To assess whether MT-RAIG Eval produces consistent results across different backbone LLMs, we analyze two open-source models (DeepSeek-R1-8B and Llama-3.1-8B) against the GPT-4o-mini, which is the original backbone of MT-RAIG Eval. Specifically, we measure how closely the evaluation scores from each open-source model align with those from GPT-4o-mini by calculating pairwise correlation. From the results in Table 8, we can observe that even when replacing the backbone with open-source models, the high correlation persists, demonstrating that our evaluation method is both reproducible and robust.

From the results in Table 6, we observe that proprietary models with larger model sizes generally show higher performance compared to open-source models that have relatively fewer parameters. To further investigate this, we conduct additional experiments by differ the parameter sizes of open-source LLM to understand the effect of model size in the table reasoning performance. Specifically, we evaluate DeepSeek-R1, which is most powerful open-source LLM among our baselines ranging from 8B to 70B parameters. From the results in Table 9, we confirm a general trend where increasing model size correlates with improved performance, with a particularly notable improvement in the faithfulness score. This discrepancy suggests that the model’s capacity to accurately interpret and reason about table structures (which is a prerequisite for faithfulness) is more directly enhanced by parameter scaling, compared to the broader task coverage implied by completeness.

To supplement Table 5, we present the Spearman correlation results alongside the Pearson correlation, in terms of faithfulness and completeness, as shown in Table 17. We report the full multi-table retrieval results in Table 19, MT-RAIG Eval Completeness score results of diverse baselines in Table 20, with respect to Precision, Recall, and F1 score across different top-k values. Additionally, we present insight generation results along with existing automatic metrics in Table 21.

We select representative examples of (1) MT-RAIG Bench in Table 22 and 23, (2) discarded data in Table 24 and 25, (3) MT-RAIG Eval Faithfulness and (4) Completeness evaluation in Table 26 and 27, respectively. Moreover, we present (5) comparison with MT-RAIG Eval results for each baseline in Table 28 and 29. Ensure that we display only the first three rows of each table to improve readability.

subsections.title: Data Annotation
paragraphs: Existing methods have primarily compute the lexical overlap (Post, 2018), or semantic similarity with the reference (Zhang et al., 2019), while some (Liu et al., 2022) focus on factuality assessment using a trained verifier to check the grounding against the source table. Some recent studies (Zhao et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024a) find that LLM-based evaluation metrics (i.e., G-Eval), show stronger alignment with human evaluation. Despite these advancements, these metrics still struggle to capture the fine-grained quality of long-form outputs. To address this gap, we propose a novel decomposition-based evaluation framework, MT-RAIG Eval, which outperforms existing metrics in aligning with human judgments.

This work introduces MT-RAIG Bench, the first large-scale benchmark for retrieval-augmented insight generation over multiple tables, alongside MT-RAIG Eval, a novel automated evaluation framework designed to address the limitations of conventional metrics in assessing multi-table insights. Extensive experiments reveal that even frontier LLMs and SOTA TableQA systems struggle to meet the challenges posed by MT-RAIG Bench.

Although we believe that our MT-RAIG Bench and MT-RAIG Eval could serve as valuable resources for the research community, several limitations remain, suggesting areas for future improvement. First, while the synthetic generation of MT-RAIG Bench enables scalable and consistent dataset construction (Tan et al., 2024; Hao et al., 2024; Han et al., 2024) as we discussed in Section 2, this approach carries inherent risks (Bender et al., 2021), such as reduced linguistic diversity in questions or potential overalignment with model generated responses that are unnatural or unfaithful description. Despite this downside, we minimize these risks by adopting a human-in-the-loop annotation process, where human experts iteratively refine machine-generated questions. Further, we conduct a machine-human dual quality control, in which automated filters first strictly discard low-quality instances, followed by human validation to ensure the quality of the final benchmark.

Second, although MT-RAIG Bench covers both relational DB tables and Wikipedia tables that constitute the two main portion of table domain, it could be beneficial to add more data sources from diverse domains (e.g., financial, scientific, or medical tables). Future works could examine how existing models perform in these specialized domains to assess their robustness to domain-specific terminology or extremely large table sets.

Lastly, a notable limitation lies in our MT-RAIG Eval’s reliance on LLM for assessment. While recent works increasingly adopt LLM-based evaluators in diverse domains due to their scalability and flexibility (Ye et al., 2024; Kim et al., 2024c; Ru et al., 2024; Han et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024a), it is important to note that their judgments may still be affected by biases or inconsistencies inherent in the backbone model’s training data or architectural design. However, we believe this issue has been largely minimized in our study. Through a meta-evaluation in Section 3, we demonstrate our MT-RAIG Eval’s reliability in aligning with human judgments. Furthermore, additional experiments that replacing the MT-RAIG Eval’s LLM backbone with open-source models (Table 17) confirm the reproducibility of our evaluation framework.

The output of text generation from LLMs may sometimes contain harmful, biased, or offensive content. However, in our research, we assert that this risk is largely minimized. The source tables and source natural language facts used in the construction of our MT-RAIG Bench are derived from SPIDER (Yu et al., 2018) and Open-WikiTable (Kweon et al., 2023), both of which are publicly available datasets licensed under the CC BY-SA 4.0, and these datasets have been annotated by human experts. Additionally, we manually reviewed the generated texts and eliminate any toxic, offensive, or biased language to ensure the quality and fairness of the auto-generated contents. For human evaluation, two graduate students participated as annotators, receiving a pre-guide before taking part in evaluation and validation. We advise the annotators to complete no more than 20 unit tasks per day, and the entire annotation process spanned approximately 30 days.
We show prompts used in data annotation process from Table 10 to 13.
To extend the existing single table corpus into a collection of multi-table sets, we classify scenarios where multiple tables are combined into two categories:
• Joinable tables: We first classify the SQL queries in the existing dataset SPIDER based on the join operator to identify tables that can be joined and then link the tables as the multi-table set.

• Topic-related tables: To cluster topically related tables, we semantically group the tables with the table meta data. Within each grouped table set, all tables are linked with related topics (at least two matching subtitles among page, section, or caption titles) and similar headers (differing by at most one column name). To consider the spatio-temporal relationships between tables, we use exact matching while excluding numeric values.
Joinable tables: We first classify the SQL queries in the existing dataset SPIDER based on the join operator to identify tables that can be joined and then link the tables as the multi-table set.

Topic-related tables: To cluster topically related tables, we semantically group the tables with the table meta data. Within each grouped table set, all tables are linked with related topics (at least two matching subtitles among page, section, or caption titles) and similar headers (differing by at most one column name). To consider the spatio-temporal relationships between tables, we use exact matching while excluding numeric values.
We provide the following definitions for each type of question to human reviewers who guide the agent with initial seed questions and validate the generated samples iteratively:

• Analysis & Summary: Synthesize multiple data sources into a coherent narrative, focusing on interpretation rather than raw figures. Identifies key metrics and contextualizes quantitative outcomes to uncover biases influencing decision-making.

• Performance & Outcome: Evaluate measurable achievements by linking performance metrics to qualitative success factors. Assesses growth and long-term accomplishments to provide a broader context for understanding progress.

• Comparison & Relationship: Analyze relationships between data points, explaining connections between attributes. Examines structural components and grouping logic to reveal organizational hierarchies and patterns, enhancing understanding of data organization.

• Trend & Pattern: Examine temporal or categorical changes to identify recurring behaviors and systemic shifts. Goes beyond documenting events to interpret transformations, providing insights into structural changes or cyclical phenomena.

Analysis & Summary: Synthesize multiple data sources into a coherent narrative, focusing on interpretation rather than raw figures. Identifies key metrics and contextualizes quantitative outcomes to uncover biases influencing decision-making.

Performance & Outcome: Evaluate measurable achievements by linking performance metrics to qualitative success factors. Assesses growth and long-term accomplishments to provide a broader context for understanding progress.

Comparison & Relationship: Analyze relationships between data points, explaining connections between attributes. Examines structural components and grouping logic to reveal organizational hierarchies and patterns, enhancing understanding of data organization.

Trend & Pattern: Examine temporal or categorical changes to identify recurring behaviors and systemic shifts. Goes beyond documenting events to interpret transformations, providing insights into structural changes or cyclical phenomena.
We first utilize an agent annotator as a self-verifier, ensuring that each multi-table set, question, and insight triple satisfies strict criteria:

• Relevance: Does the question appropriately capture the relationships between the tables in the multi-table set, ensuring that it pertains to all tables and can be answered solely using the provided information?
• Faithfulness: Does the insight accurately reflect the information within the multi-table set, ensuring it remains grounded in the given data while providing a clear and unambiguous response?
• Completeness: Does the insight fully and logically address the question, covering all necessary aspects while maintaining clarity and coherence?

Relevance: Does the question appropriately capture the relationships between the tables in the multi-table set, ensuring that it pertains to all tables and can be answered solely using the provided information?
Faithfulness: Does the insight accurately reflect the information within the multi-table set, ensuring it remains grounded in the given data while providing a clear and unambiguous response?
Completeness: Does the insight fully and logically address the question, covering all necessary aspects while maintaining clarity and coherence?

Any triple (multi table set, question, and insight) that fails at least one of these standards is discarded. Representative examples of discarded data are in Table 24 and 25. We report the statistics of self-verification process in Table 7. Prompts used in agent verification process for three criteria are shown in Table 14.

Alongside the agent verification, we incorporate human validation as critical review points for the annotated data, balancing the efficiency of automated annotation with the reliability of human annotation to ensure the quality of MT-RAIG BENCH. We provide the human validation interface in Figure 6. Specifically, we adopt the following criteria to check the quality of table,question,and insight triples:
• Inter-Table Relevance: Measures how effectively data from different tables can be connected or combined to provide comprehensive insights.
• Table-Question Relevance:Assesses the extent to which the contents of a table directly support and address the given question.
• Quesiton Complexity :Evaluates the level of difficulty and the number of factors or layers involved in understanding or answering the question.
• Question Meaningfulness:Determines whether the question is clearly defined, significant, and natural.
• Question-Insight Completeness: Checks if the insight provides all the necessary key information to address the question.
• Table-Insight Faithfulness:Ensures that the insights drawn accurately and reliably reflect the source tables.

Question-Insight Completeness: Checks if the insight provides all the necessary key information to address the question.
Table-Insight Faithfulness:Ensures that the insights drawn accurately and reliably reflect the source tables.

Considering that all automatic metrics are designed with distinct objectives and functionalities, direct numerical comparisons between their scores are inherently limited. Instead, it is intuitive that a metric’s reliability should be judged by its capacity to align with relative human preferences. To operationalize this principle, we adopt the meta-evaluation protocol proposed by Ru et al. (2024), constructing a meta evaluation dataset of 250 response pairs sampled from 11 different baseline generators. Human evaluators are then tasked with annotating each pair across two dimensions—faithfulness and completeness—by selecting one of three options: win, tie, or loss. The annotation interface is illustrated in Figure 7. We implement MT-RAIG Eval on the top of gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18. To check the reproducibility of MT-RAIG Eval, we additionally conduct an ablation study in Table 21, examining the impact of different backbone LLMs on MT-RAIG Eval. • METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) evaluates text similarity by using unigram matching between machine-generated outputs and human reference texts.
• BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2019) computes the similarity between generated and reference texts using contextual word embeddings.
• A3CU (Liu et al., 2023b) evaluates summarization quality by directly comparing texts without extracting atomic content units, providing a human-aligned assessment of content similarity.

• TAPAS-Acc (Liu et al., 2022) is a reference-free metric that uses a TAPAS (Herzig et al., 2020) model fine-tuned on the TabFact Chen et al. (2019) dataset to assess the faithfulness of generated text by verifying factual consistency.

• G-Eval (Liu et al., 2023a) assesses the quality of generated text based on specific evaluation criteria using LLMs. We adopt G-Eval to separately evaluate faithfulness and completeness on a 5-point Likert scale. The evaluation prompts are provided in Table 18.

DeepSeek-R1-8B (DeepSeek-AI, 2025) is distilled version of DeepSeek-R1, which is a open-source LLM released by DeepSeek AI. In this study, we utilize unsloth/DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama -8B, which is based on Llama-3.1-8B.
Qwen2-7B (Yang et al., 2024) is an open-source LLM developed by Alibaba Cloud, with the largest model containing 72 billion parameters. In this study, we utilize Qwen/Qwen2-7B- Instruct.

Gemma-7B (Mesnard et al., 2024) is an open-source LLM developed by Google DeepMind, which is known for its multilingual capabilities and creative outputs. In this study, we utilize google/gemma-7b-it.
Llama 3.1-8B (MetaAI, 2024) is an open-source LLM released by Meta AI, which is available in multiple sizes up to 405 billion parameters. In this study, we utilize meta-llama/Llama- 3.1-8B-Instruct.
Mistral-7B (Jiang et al., 2023) is an open-source LLM developed by Mistral AI. In this study, we utilize mistralai/Mistral-7B- Instruct-v0.3.
• Chain-of-Table (Wang et al., 2024b) is LLM-based method designed to enhance table-based reasoning. It employs iterative reconstruction of input table through dynamic tabular operations.

• TaPERA (Zhao et al., 2024) a modular framework designed to enhance faithfulness and interpretability in long-form table question answering by combining a QA-based content planner and execution-based reasoning..
• Dater (Ye et al., 2023) focuses on selecting relevant information from input tables and providing contextual information to support the statement verification process.
Chain-of-Table (Wang et al., 2024b) is LLM-based method designed to enhance table-based reasoning. It employs iterative reconstruction of input table through dynamic tabular operations.

TaPERA (Zhao et al., 2024) a modular framework designed to enhance faithfulness and interpretability in long-form table question answering by combining a QA-based content planner and execution-based reasoning..
Dater (Ye et al., 2023) focuses on selecting relevant information from input tables and providing contextual information to support the statement verification process.

Following recent studies that utilize language models for table-related tasks (Chen, 2023; Seo et al., 2024), we serialize the table input into a flattened sequence to effectively represent table data for language model processing. The table title is enclosed within [TITLE] tags, followed by the table headers marked with [HEADER], where individual column names are separated by a vertical bar (|). Each row is prefixed with a [ROW] tag and an index, while cell values are separated by a vertical bar. This approach ensures that the table format is preserved while making the input compatible with language models. For example, the input table is formatted as follows:
[TITLE] title [HEADER] col 1 | col2 | ... [ROW 1] cell 1,1 | cell 1,2 | ... [ROW 2] cell 2,1 | ...

For the table-specific retriever, we fine-tune each model on a single-table QA dataset using the AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of for 5 epochs. To efficiently finetune the model, we adopt LoRA and set the parameters as rr = 8, α\alpha = 32. We use a constant learning rate schedule set at 2e-5, and train with the batch size of 1 on 4 NVIDIA A100 GPU. The inference of open-source LLMs are conducted using vLLM framework (Kwon et al., 2023). We set temperature to 0.0 for efficient and robust output generation.

To assess whether MT-RAIG Eval produces consistent results across different backbone LLMs, we analyze two open-source models (DeepSeek-R1-8B and Llama-3.1-8B) against the GPT-4o-mini, which is the original backbone of MT-RAIG Eval. Specifically, we measure how closely the evaluation scores from each open-source model align with those from GPT-4o-mini by calculating pairwise correlation. From the results in Table 8, we can observe that even when replacing the backbone with open-source models, the high correlation persists, demonstrating that our evaluation method is both reproducible and robust.

From the results in Table 6, we observe that proprietary models with larger model sizes generally show higher performance compared to open-source models that have relatively fewer parameters. To further investigate this, we conduct additional experiments by differ the parameter sizes of open-source LLM to understand the effect of model size in the table reasoning performance. Specifically, we evaluate DeepSeek-R1, which is most powerful open-source LLM among our baselines ranging from 8B to 70B parameters. From the results in Table 9, we confirm a general trend where increasing model size correlates with improved performance, with a particularly notable improvement in the faithfulness score. This discrepancy suggests that the model’s capacity to accurately interpret and reason about table structures (which is a prerequisite for faithfulness) is more directly enhanced by parameter scaling, compared to the broader task coverage implied by completeness.

To supplement Table 5, we present the Spearman correlation results alongside the Pearson correlation, in terms of faithfulness and completeness, as shown in Table 17. We report the full multi-table retrieval results in Table 19, MT-RAIG Eval Completeness score results of diverse baselines in Table 20, with respect to Precision, Recall, and F1 score across different top-k values. Additionally, we present insight generation results along with existing automatic metrics in Table 21.

We select representative examples of (1) MT-RAIG Bench in Table 22 and 23, (2) discarded data in Table 24 and 25, (3) MT-RAIG Eval Faithfulness and (4) Completeness evaluation in Table 26 and 27, respectively. Moreover, we present (5) comparison with MT-RAIG Eval results for each baseline in Table 28 and 29. Ensure that we display only the first three rows of each table to improve readability.

subsections.title: Data Annotation
paragraphs: We show prompts used in data annotation process from Table 10 to 13.
To extend the existing single table corpus into a collection of multi-table sets, we classify scenarios where multiple tables are combined into two categories:
• Joinable tables: We first classify the SQL queries in the existing dataset SPIDER based on the join operator to identify tables that can be joined and then link the tables as the multi-table set.

• Topic-related tables: To cluster topically related tables, we semantically group the tables with the table meta data. Within each grouped table set, all tables are linked with related topics (at least two matching subtitles among page, section, or caption titles) and similar headers (differing by at most one column name). To consider the spatio-temporal relationships between tables, we use exact matching while excluding numeric values.
Joinable tables: We first classify the SQL queries in the existing dataset SPIDER based on the join operator to identify tables that can be joined and then link the tables as the multi-table set.

Topic-related tables: To cluster topically related tables, we semantically group the tables with the table meta data. Within each grouped table set, all tables are linked with related topics (at least two matching subtitles among page, section, or caption titles) and similar headers (differing by at most one column name). To consider the spatio-temporal relationships between tables, we use exact matching while excluding numeric values.
We provide the following definitions for each type of question to human reviewers who guide the agent with initial seed questions and validate the generated samples iteratively:

• Analysis & Summary: Synthesize multiple data sources into a coherent narrative, focusing on interpretation rather than raw figures. Identifies key metrics and contextualizes quantitative outcomes to uncover biases influencing decision-making.

• Performance & Outcome: Evaluate measurable achievements by linking performance metrics to qualitative success factors. Assesses growth and long-term accomplishments to provide a broader context for understanding progress.

• Comparison & Relationship: Analyze relationships between data points, explaining connections between attributes. Examines structural components and grouping logic to reveal organizational hierarchies and patterns, enhancing understanding of data organization.

• Trend & Pattern: Examine temporal or categorical changes to identify recurring behaviors and systemic shifts. Goes beyond documenting events to interpret transformations, providing insights into structural changes or cyclical phenomena.

Performance & Outcome: Evaluate measurable achievements by linking performance metrics to qualitative success factors. Assesses growth and long-term accomplishments to provide a broader context for understanding progress.

subsections.title: Quality Control
paragraphs: We first utilize an agent annotator as a self-verifier, ensuring that each multi-table set, question, and insight triple satisfies strict criteria:

• Relevance: Does the question appropriately capture the relationships between the tables in the multi-table set, ensuring that it pertains to all tables and can be answered solely using the provided information?
• Faithfulness: Does the insight accurately reflect the information within the multi-table set, ensuring it remains grounded in the given data while providing a clear and unambiguous response?
• Completeness: Does the insight fully and logically address the question, covering all necessary aspects while maintaining clarity and coherence?

Relevance: Does the question appropriately capture the relationships between the tables in the multi-table set, ensuring that it pertains to all tables and can be answered solely using the provided information?
Faithfulness: Does the insight accurately reflect the information within the multi-table set, ensuring it remains grounded in the given data while providing a clear and unambiguous response?
Completeness: Does the insight fully and logically address the question, covering all necessary aspects while maintaining clarity and coherence?

Any triple (multi table set, question, and insight) that fails at least one of these standards is discarded. Representative examples of discarded data are in Table 24 and 25. We report the statistics of self-verification process in Table 7. Prompts used in agent verification process for three criteria are shown in Table 14.

Alongside the agent verification, we incorporate human validation as critical review points for the annotated data, balancing the efficiency of automated annotation with the reliability of human annotation to ensure the quality of MT-RAIG BENCH. We provide the human validation interface in Figure 6. Specifically, we adopt the following criteria to check the quality of table,question,and insight triples:
• Inter-Table Relevance: Measures how effectively data from different tables can be connected or combined to provide comprehensive insights.
• Table-Question Relevance:Assesses the extent to which the contents of a table directly support and address the given question.
• Quesiton Complexity :Evaluates the level of difficulty and the number of factors or layers involved in understanding or answering the question.
• Question Meaningfulness:Determines whether the question is clearly defined, significant, and natural.
• Question-Insight Completeness: Checks if the insight provides all the necessary key information to address the question.
• Table-Insight Faithfulness:Ensures that the insights drawn accurately and reliably reflect the source tables.

subsections.title: Meta Evaluation
paragraphs: Considering that all automatic metrics are designed with distinct objectives and functionalities, direct numerical comparisons between their scores are inherently limited. Instead, it is intuitive that a metric’s reliability should be judged by its capacity to align with relative human preferences. To operationalize this principle, we adopt the meta-evaluation protocol proposed by Ru et al. (2024), constructing a meta evaluation dataset of 250 response pairs sampled from 11 different baseline generators. Human evaluators are then tasked with annotating each pair across two dimensions—faithfulness and completeness—by selecting one of three options: win, tie, or loss. The annotation interface is illustrated in Figure 7. We implement MT-RAIG Eval on the top of gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18. To check the reproducibility of MT-RAIG Eval, we additionally conduct an ablation study in Table 21, examining the impact of different backbone LLMs on MT-RAIG Eval. We consider the following automatic metrics as baselines:

• SacreBLEU (Post, 2018) standardizes BLEU Papineni et al. (2002) score calculations by ensuring consistent and reproducible results. It measures the geometric mean of n-gram precision over the output text.
• ROUGE-L (Lin and Hovy, 2003) evaluates text similarity based on the longest common subsequence. Specifically, we reported F1 score.
• METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) evaluates text similarity by using unigram matching between machine-generated outputs and human reference texts.
• BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2019) computes the similarity between generated and reference texts using contextual word embeddings.
• A3CU (Liu et al., 2023b) evaluates summarization quality by directly comparing texts without extracting atomic content units, providing a human-aligned assessment of content similarity.

• TAPAS-Acc (Liu et al., 2022) is a reference-free metric that uses a TAPAS (Herzig et al., 2020) model fine-tuned on the TabFact Chen et al. (2019) dataset to assess the faithfulness of generated text by verifying factual consistency.

• G-Eval (Liu et al., 2023a) assesses the quality of generated text based on specific evaluation criteria using LLMs. We adopt G-Eval to separately evaluate faithfulness and completeness on a 5-point Likert scale. The evaluation prompts are provided in Table 18.

subsections.title: Retrieval Baselines
paragraphs: To assess the effectiveness of multi-table retrieval, we evaluate both general-purpose retrievers and table-specific retrievers. General-purpose retrievers serve as strong baselines, given their widespread use in plain retrieval tasks. Table-specific retrievers are designed explicitly for tables, leveraging table-specific representations for improved retrieval performance. Specifically, we consider the following retrievers as the baselines:

• BM25 (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009) is a sparse retriever that relies on a traditional bag-of-words representation to score relevant documents. In this study, we rank linearized tables as documents based on term frequency and inverse document frequency, leveraging lexical overlaps between questions and tables.

• DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020) employs BERT-based encoders to independently map questions and documents into a shared embedding space. It learns dense vector representations that enable semantic similarity matching. In this study, we implement DPR utilizing facebook/dpr- encoder-multiset-base.

• Contriever (Izacard et al., 2021) encodes questions and documents into a shared embedding space, optimizing for relevance through contrastive learning. In this study, we implement Contriever utilizing facebook/ contriever-msmacro.

Contriever (Izacard et al., 2021) encodes questions and documents into a shared embedding space, optimizing for relevance through contrastive learning. In this study, we implement Contriever utilizing facebook/ contriever-msmacro.

• DTR (Herzig et al., 2021) is a table-specific dense retriever built on a TAPAS (Herzig et al., 2020) backbone, designed to effectively encode tabular structures and relationships. In this study, we fine-tune google/tapas-base.

• TableLlama (Zhang et al., 2023) is Llama 2-7B (Touvron et al., 2023) based open-source LLM-based generalist model that is designed for a variety of table-based tasks. We utilize TableLlama to generate question and table embeddings for table retrieval task. In this study, we fine-tune osunlp/TableLlama.

We utilize TableLlama to generate question and table embeddings for table retrieval task. In this study, we fine-tune osunlp/TableLlama.

subsections.title: Generator Baselines
paragraphs: To comprehensively compare the performance of insight generation across multiple tables, we consider three types of baselines: proprietary LLMs, open-source LLMs, and SOTA table question-answering methods. Proprietary LLMs encompass commercial models renowned for their advanced reasoning and high-quality text generation capabilities. Open-source LLMs, serving as transparent and adaptable alternatives, leverage large-scale training data and advanced architectures to deliver performance that is competitive with proprietary counterparts. SOTA TQA methods are specialized approaches explicitly engineered for complex, table-aware reasoning tasks, prioritizing accuracy in tabular question-answering. We consider the following models as the baseline generators:

• o3-mini (OpenAI, 2025b) is a smaller yet advanced LLM developed by OpenAI, which is designed to efficiently solve complex problems by breaking them into constituent parts. In this study, we leverage o3-mini-2025-01-31 with reasoning effort parameter as medium.

• GPT-4o (OpenAI, 2024) is an advanced OpenAI’s proprietary LLM known for its enhanced reasoning capabilities and performance across various disciplines. In this study, we leverage gpt-4o-2024-08-06 checkpoint.

• Claude 3.5 Sonnet (Anthropic, ) is developed by Anthropic. It features improvements in coding proficiency and multimodal capabilities. In this study, we leverage claude-3-5-sonnet -20241022 checkpoint.

subsections.title: Implementation Details
paragraphs: Following recent studies that utilize language models for table-related tasks (Chen, 2023; Seo et al., 2024), we serialize the table input into a flattened sequence to effectively represent table data for language model processing. The table title is enclosed within [TITLE] tags, followed by the table headers marked with [HEADER], where individual column names are separated by a vertical bar (|). Each row is prefixed with a [ROW] tag and an index, while cell values are separated by a vertical bar. This approach ensures that the table format is preserved while making the input compatible with language models. For example, the input table is formatted as follows:
[TITLE] title [HEADER] col 1 | col2 | ... [ROW 1] cell 1,1 | cell 1,2 | ... [ROW 2] cell 2,1 | ...

For the table-specific retriever, we fine-tune each model on a single-table QA dataset using the AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of for 5 epochs. To efficiently finetune the model, we adopt LoRA and set the parameters as rr = 8, α\alpha = 32. We use a constant learning rate schedule set at 2e-5, and train with the batch size of 1 on 4 NVIDIA A100 GPU. The inference of open-source LLMs are conducted using vLLM framework (Kwon et al., 2023). We set temperature to 0.0 for efficient and robust output generation.

subsections.title: Reproducibility ofMT-RAIG EvalBackbone
paragraphs: To assess whether MT-RAIG Eval produces consistent results across different backbone LLMs, we analyze two open-source models (DeepSeek-R1-8B and Llama-3.1-8B) against the GPT-4o-mini, which is the original backbone of MT-RAIG Eval. Specifically, we measure how closely the evaluation scores from each open-source model align with those from GPT-4o-mini by calculating pairwise correlation. From the results in Table 8, we can observe that even when replacing the backbone with open-source models, the high correlation persists, demonstrating that our evaluation method is both reproducible and robust.

subsections.title: Effect of Parameter Scaling onMT-RAIG BenchPerformance
paragraphs: From the results in Table 6, we observe that proprietary models with larger model sizes generally show higher performance compared to open-source models that have relatively fewer parameters. To further investigate this, we conduct additional experiments by differ the parameter sizes of open-source LLM to understand the effect of model size in the table reasoning performance. Specifically, we evaluate DeepSeek-R1, which is most powerful open-source LLM among our baselines ranging from 8B to 70B parameters. From the results in Table 9, we confirm a general trend where increasing model size correlates with improved performance, with a particularly notable improvement in the faithfulness score. This discrepancy suggests that the model’s capacity to accurately interpret and reason about table structures (which is a prerequisite for faithfulness) is more directly enhanced by parameter scaling, compared to the broader task coverage implied by completeness.

subsections.title: Baselines
paragraphs: We benchmark the performance of diverse baselines on MT-RAIG Bench. For multi-table retrieval, we consider both general-purpose retriever (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009; Karpukhin et al., 2020; Izacard et al., 2021), and table-specific retriever (Herzig et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2023) as the baselines. For insight generation, we employ 11 LLM-based baselines including proprietary (OpenAI, 2025b, 2024; Anthropic, ), open-source (DeepSeek-AI, 2025; Yang et al., 2024; Mesnard et al., 2024; MetaAI, 2024; Jiang et al., 2023), and SOTA-TQA methods (Ye et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2024b; Zhao et al., 2024) for comprehensive evaluations. The results are shown in Table 4 and Table 6, respectively. Please refer to Appendix B for the detailed information on the experimental setup and all the baselines.

subsections.title: Results
paragraphs: To understand the challenges of multi-table retrieval in MT-RAIG Bench, we compare the performance of five widely-used retrievers. From the results in Table 4, we observe that there remains a significant gap between high and low top-kk performance, which is critical for the generation performance given that LLMs often hold only a limited number of tables in their context window. We also find that table-specific embedding models do not improve retrieval performance on MT-RAIG Bench. Instead, general text-based embedding model achieves better performance, similar to the findings of Wang et al. (2022). We attribute this to the nature of the MT-RAIG task, which prioritizes identifying insight-level semantic connection of tables for multi-hop queries rather than understanding the structural characteristics of tables. Based on these findings, we use DPR’s top-10 retrieved tables (considering the LLM’s context window limitation) for experiments in Table 5 and Table 6.

We conduct a meta evaluation to assess the reliability of MT-RAIG Eval against existing automatic metrics. Specifically, we construct a meta evaluation dataset comprising 250 pairs of responses sampled from baseline generators, where each pair is labeled by two human evaluators based on their relative preferences on response faithfulness and completeness. For scoring, we follow the setting of Ru et al. (2024) to normalize each auto-evaluation score difference to the human preference scale of [-1,0,1] and then measure the Pearson correlation with human preference ratings. Additionally, we report the correlation between the human evaluators as the upper bound. From the results in Table 5, we can observe that MT-RAIG Eval achieves the highest correlation with human preference ratings across both dimensions, demonstrating its reliability over baseline methods for evaluating MT-RAIG Bench. We provide more detailed results in Appendix D.

Leveraging the MT-RAIG Eval, we evaluate the insight generation performance of various baselines on MT-RAIG Bench. From the results in Table 6 and Figure 4, we derive the following key conclusions: (1) MT-RAIG Bench poses a significant challenge for insight-level table reasoning, even for frontier LLMs. Both open-source and proprietary models struggle to generate insights for questions in MT-RAIG Bench, achieving only around 40% in faithfulness and 60% in completeness even when provided with gold tables TqT_{q} as input. (2) Deep thinking with scaling test-time compute also shows promise in insight-level table reasoning tasks. Although proprietary models generally outperform open-source counterparts, DeepSeek-R1-8b achieves performance on par with proprietary models and even surpasses Claude and o3-mini in completeness score, despite having a smaller number of parameters. Given that a similar reasoning model, o3-mini, also demonstrates strong performance, these results supports OpenAI (2025a); DeepSeek-AI (2025)’s recent finding that inference-time scaling substantially enhances models’ capacity for complex reasoning, suggesting its applicability can also extend to insight-level table reasoning tasks. (3) SOTA TQA-methods underperform compared to general-purpose LLMs. We attribute this to the focus of existing TQA methods on single-table factoid extraction and their specialization in closed-domain scenarios, which limits their robustness to noise from irrelevant tables.

subsections.title: Further Analysis
paragraphs: Trade-offs in retrieving more tables We first analyze the relationship between retrieval and generation performance by varying the number of retrieved tables (k). While increasing the number of k is an intuitive way to improve generation performance in RAG systems (Kim et al., 2024a), we investigate whether this also holds for the table domain, where retrieved information is structurally more complex than general text. From the results in Figure 5 (Upper), we first observe that the generation performance broadly aligns with conventional intuition, exhibiting an overall improvement across all models as k increases. However, we also find that a continued increase in k does not guarantee sustained performance gains. Beyond a certain threshold, performance plateaus or even declines, suggesting that retrieving an excessive number of tables introduces noise into the generation process.

Guided by the observations in Figure 5 (Upper), we further investigate how sensitively the generator responds to noisy table information. To isolate the impact of noise from retrieval errors, we analyze samples where the entire gold table set is successfully retrieved. Within these samples, we analyze the performance by varying the ratio of irrelevant to gold tables while keeping the gold tables fixed as part of the model input. The results in Figure 5 (Middle) reveal that, as the proportion of irrelevant tables increases, faithfulness shows a marked decrease while completeness remains relatively stable. This suggests that although including the correct tables ensures the generator can somewhat address the high-level key steps (i.e., maintain completeness), the added noise from irrelevant tables hinders the extraction of precise factors for the final insights.

While our findings indicate that generation performance degrades with noisy tables, one might wonder how challenging it becomes for the model as the number of source tables it should handle increases, even in noise-free conditions. To explore this, we conduct a closed-domain experiment in which only the gold tables are provided as input, and investigate how generation performance varies depending on the number of tables in the gold table set that the model should reference. From the results in Figure 5 (Lower), we observe a significant drop in both faithfulness and completeness as the number of gold tables to reference grows, showing that current models struggle to conduct complex reasoning across multiple sources of tables.

Benchmarks for table-based reasoning has been studied across several major tasks, including text-to-SQL (Zhong et al., 2018), table-to-text (Parikh et al., 2020), and tableQA (Pasupat and Liang, 2015a). Early works (Yu et al., 2018; Nan et al., 2021) mainly focus on measuring the performance of neural models as executors for extracting factoid-level information, where models generate SQL queries or direct answers from tables. More recently, some works (Zhao et al., 2023a; Seo et al., 2024) target insight-level table reasoning tasks, moving beyond extracting explicit information from tables. Unlike prior works, our MT-RAIG Bench significantly differs by exploring more realistic scenario where the system retrieves multiple evidence tables to generate insightful responses.

Existing methods have primarily compute the lexical overlap (Post, 2018), or semantic similarity with the reference (Zhang et al., 2019), while some (Liu et al., 2022) focus on factuality assessment using a trained verifier to check the grounding against the source table. Some recent studies (Zhao et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024a) find that LLM-based evaluation metrics (i.e., G-Eval), show stronger alignment with human evaluation. Despite these advancements, these metrics still struggle to capture the fine-grained quality of long-form outputs. To address this gap, we propose a novel decomposition-based evaluation framework, MT-RAIG Eval, which outperforms existing metrics in aligning with human judgments.

This work introduces MT-RAIG Bench, the first large-scale benchmark for retrieval-augmented insight generation over multiple tables, alongside MT-RAIG Eval, a novel automated evaluation framework designed to address the limitations of conventional metrics in assessing multi-table insights. Extensive experiments reveal that even frontier LLMs and SOTA TableQA systems struggle to meet the challenges posed by MT-RAIG Bench.

Although we believe that our MT-RAIG Bench and MT-RAIG Eval could serve as valuable resources for the research community, several limitations remain, suggesting areas for future improvement. First, while the synthetic generation of MT-RAIG Bench enables scalable and consistent dataset construction (Tan et al., 2024; Hao et al., 2024; Han et al., 2024) as we discussed in Section 2, this approach carries inherent risks (Bender et al., 2021), such as reduced linguistic diversity in questions or potential overalignment with model generated responses that are unnatural or unfaithful description. Despite this downside, we minimize these risks by adopting a human-in-the-loop annotation process, where human experts iteratively refine machine-generated questions. Further, we conduct a machine-human dual quality control, in which automated filters first strictly discard low-quality instances, followed by human validation to ensure the quality of the final benchmark.

Second, although MT-RAIG Bench covers both relational DB tables and Wikipedia tables that constitute the two main portion of table domain, it could be beneficial to add more data sources from diverse domains (e.g., financial, scientific, or medical tables). Future works could examine how existing models perform in these specialized domains to assess their robustness to domain-specific terminology or extremely large table sets.

Lastly, a notable limitation lies in our MT-RAIG Eval’s reliance on LLM for assessment. While recent works increasingly adopt LLM-based evaluators in diverse domains due to their scalability and flexibility (Ye et al., 2024; Kim et al., 2024c; Ru et al., 2024; Han et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024a), it is important to note that their judgments may still be affected by biases or inconsistencies inherent in the backbone model’s training data or architectural design. However, we believe this issue has been largely minimized in our study. Through a meta-evaluation in Section 3, we demonstrate our MT-RAIG Eval’s reliability in aligning with human judgments. Furthermore, additional experiments that replacing the MT-RAIG Eval’s LLM backbone with open-source models (Table 17) confirm the reproducibility of our evaluation framework.

The output of text generation from LLMs may sometimes contain harmful, biased, or offensive content. However, in our research, we assert that this risk is largely minimized. The source tables and source natural language facts used in the construction of our MT-RAIG Bench are derived from SPIDER (Yu et al., 2018) and Open-WikiTable (Kweon et al., 2023), both of which are publicly available datasets licensed under the CC BY-SA 4.0, and these datasets have been annotated by human experts. Additionally, we manually reviewed the generated texts and eliminate any toxic, offensive, or biased language to ensure the quality and fairness of the auto-generated contents. For human evaluation, two graduate students participated as annotators, receiving a pre-guide before taking part in evaluation and validation. We advise the annotators to complete no more than 20 unit tasks per day, and the entire annotation process spanned approximately 30 days.
We show prompts used in data annotation process from Table 10 to 13.
To extend the existing single table corpus into a collection of multi-table sets, we classify scenarios where multiple tables are combined into two categories:
• Joinable tables: We first classify the SQL queries in the existing dataset SPIDER based on the join operator to identify tables that can be joined and then link the tables as the multi-table set.

• Topic-related tables: To cluster topically related tables, we semantically group the tables with the table meta data. Within each grouped table set, all tables are linked with related topics (at least two matching subtitles among page, section, or caption titles) and similar headers (differing by at most one column name). To consider the spatio-temporal relationships between tables, we use exact matching while excluding numeric values.

We provide the following definitions for each type of question to human reviewers who guide the agent with initial seed questions and validate the generated samples iteratively:

• Analysis & Summary: Synthesize multiple data sources into a coherent narrative, focusing on interpretation rather than raw figures. Identifies key metrics and contextualizes quantitative outcomes to uncover biases influencing decision-making.

• Performance & Outcome: Evaluate measurable achievements by linking performance metrics to qualitative success factors. Assesses growth and long-term accomplishments to provide a broader context for understanding progress.

• Comparison & Relationship: Analyze relationships between data points, explaining connections between attributes. Examines structural components and grouping logic to reveal organizational hierarchies and patterns, enhancing understanding of data organization.

• Trend & Pattern: Examine temporal or categorical changes to identify recurring behaviors and systemic shifts. Goes beyond documenting events to interpret transformations, providing insights into structural changes or cyclical phenomena.

Performance & Outcome: Evaluate measurable achievements by linking performance metrics to qualitative success factors. Assesses growth and long-term accomplishments to provide a broader context for understanding progress.

We first utilize an agent annotator as a self-verifier, ensuring that each multi-table set, question, and insight triple satisfies strict criteria:

• Relevance: Does the question appropriately capture the relationships between the tables in the multi-table set, ensuring that it pertains to all tables and can be answered solely using the provided information?
• Faithfulness: Does the insight accurately reflect the information within the multi-table set, ensuring it remains grounded in the given data while providing a clear and unambiguous response?
• Completeness: Does the insight fully and logically address the question, covering all necessary aspects while maintaining clarity and coherence?

Relevance: Does the question appropriately capture the relationships between the tables in the multi-table set, ensuring that it pertains to all tables and can be answered solely using the provided information?
Faithfulness: Does the insight accurately reflect the information within the multi-table set, ensuring it remains grounded in the given data while providing a clear and unambiguous response?
Completeness: Does the insight fully and logically address the question, covering all necessary aspects while maintaining clarity and coherence?

Any triple (multi table set, question, and insight) that fails at least one of these standards is discarded. Representative examples of discarded data are in Table 24 and 25. We report the statistics of self-verification process in Table 7. Prompts used in agent verification process for three criteria are shown in Table 14.

Alongside the agent verification, we incorporate human validation as critical review points for the annotated data, balancing the efficiency of automated annotation with the reliability of human annotation to ensure the quality of MT-RAIG BENCH. We provide the human validation interface in Figure 6. Specifically, we adopt the following criteria to check the quality of table,question,and insight triples:
• Inter-Table Relevance: Measures how effectively data from different tables can be connected or combined to provide comprehensive insights.
• Table-Question Relevance:Assesses the extent to which the contents of a table directly support and address the given question.
• Quesiton Complexity :Evaluates the level of difficulty and the number of factors or layers involved in understanding or answering the question.
• Question Meaningfulness:Determines whether the question is clearly defined, significant, and natural.
• Question-Insight Completeness: Checks if the insight provides all the necessary key information to address the question.
• Table-Insight Faithfulness:Ensures that the insights drawn accurately and reliably reflect the source tables.
Inter-Table Relevance: Measures how effectively data from different tables can be connected or combined to provide comprehensive insights.
Table-Question Relevance:Assesses the extent to which the contents of a table directly support and address the given question.

Question Meaningfulness:Determines whether the question is clearly defined, significant, and natural.
Question-Insight Completeness: Checks if the insight provides all the necessary key information to address the question.
Table-Insight Faithfulness:Ensures that the insights drawn accurately and reliably reflect the source tables.

Considering that all automatic metrics are designed with distinct objectives and functionalities, direct numerical comparisons between their scores are inherently limited. Instead, it is intuitive that a metric’s reliability should be judged by its capacity to align with relative human preferences. To operationalize this principle, we adopt the meta-evaluation protocol proposed by Ru et al. (2024), constructing a meta evaluation dataset of 250 response pairs sampled from 11 different baseline generators. Human evaluators are then tasked with annotating each pair across two dimensions—faithfulness and completeness—by selecting one of three options: win, tie, or loss. The annotation interface is illustrated in Figure 7. We implement MT-RAIG Eval on the top of gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18. To check the reproducibility of MT-RAIG Eval, we additionally conduct an ablation study in Table 21, examining the impact of different backbone LLMs on MT-RAIG Eval. We consider the following automatic metrics as baselines:

• SacreBLEU (Post, 2018) standardizes BLEU Papineni et al. (2002) score calculations by ensuring consistent and reproducible results. It measures the geometric mean of n-gram precision over the output text.
• ROUGE-L (Lin and Hovy, 2003) evaluates text similarity based on the longest common subsequence. Specifically, we reported F1 score.
• METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) evaluates text similarity by using unigram matching between machine-generated outputs and human reference texts.
• BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2019) computes the similarity between generated and reference texts using contextual word embeddings.
• A3CU (Liu et al., 2023b) evaluates summarization quality by directly comparing texts without extracting atomic content units, providing a human-aligned assessment of content similarity.

• TAPAS-Acc (Liu et al., 2022) is a reference-free metric that uses a TAPAS (Herzig et al., 2020) model fine-tuned on the TabFact Chen et al. (2019) dataset to assess the faithfulness of generated text by verifying factual consistency.

• G-Eval (Liu et al., 2023a) assesses the quality of generated text based on specific evaluation criteria using LLMs. We adopt G-Eval to separately evaluate faithfulness and completeness on a 5-point Likert scale. The evaluation prompts are provided in Table 18.

SacreBLEU (Post, 2018) standardizes BLEU Papineni et al. (2002) score calculations by ensuring consistent and reproducible results. It measures the geometric mean of n-gram precision over the output text.

METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) evaluates text similarity by using unigram matching between machine-generated outputs and human reference texts.

G-Eval (Liu et al., 2023a) assesses the quality of generated text based on specific evaluation criteria using LLMs. We adopt G-Eval to separately evaluate faithfulness and completeness on a 5-point Likert scale. The evaluation prompts are provided in Table 18.

To assess the effectiveness of multi-table retrieval, we evaluate both general-purpose retrievers and table-specific retrievers. General-purpose retrievers serve as strong baselines, given their widespread use in plain retrieval tasks. Table-specific retrievers are designed explicitly for tables, leveraging table-specific representations for improved retrieval performance. Specifically, we consider the following retrievers as the baselines:

• BM25 (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009) is a sparse retriever that relies on a traditional bag-of-words representation to score relevant documents. In this study, we rank linearized tables as documents based on term frequency and inverse document frequency, leveraging lexical overlaps between questions and tables.

• DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020) employs BERT-based encoders to independently map questions and documents into a shared embedding space. It learns dense vector representations that enable semantic similarity matching. In this study, we implement DPR utilizing facebook/dpr- encoder-multiset-base.

• Contriever (Izacard et al., 2021) encodes questions and documents into a shared embedding space, optimizing for relevance through contrastive learning. In this study, we implement Contriever utilizing facebook/ contriever-msmacro.

BM25 (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009) is a sparse retriever that relies on a traditional bag-of-words representation to score relevant documents. In this study, we rank linearized tables as documents based on term frequency and inverse document frequency, leveraging lexical overlaps between questions and tables.

DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020) employs BERT-based encoders to independently map questions and documents into a shared embedding space. It learns dense vector representations that enable semantic similarity matching. In this study, we implement DPR utilizing facebook/dpr- encoder-multiset-base.

Contriever (Izacard et al., 2021) encodes questions and documents into a shared embedding space, optimizing for relevance through contrastive learning. In this study, we implement Contriever utilizing facebook/ contriever-msmacro.

• DTR (Herzig et al., 2021) is a table-specific dense retriever built on a TAPAS (Herzig et al., 2020) backbone, designed to effectively encode tabular structures and relationships. In this study, we fine-tune google/tapas-base.

• TableLlama (Zhang et al., 2023) is Llama 2-7B (Touvron et al., 2023) based open-source LLM-based generalist model that is designed for a variety of table-based tasks. We utilize TableLlama to generate question and table embeddings for table retrieval task. In this study, we fine-tune osunlp/TableLlama.

DTR (Herzig et al., 2021) is a table-specific dense retriever built on a TAPAS (Herzig et al., 2020) backbone, designed to effectively encode tabular structures and relationships. In this study, we fine-tune google/tapas-base.

TableLlama (Zhang et al., 2023) is Llama 2-7B (Touvron et al., 2023) based open-source LLM-based generalist model that is designed for a variety of table-based tasks. We utilize TableLlama to generate question and table embeddings for table retrieval task. In this study, we fine-tune osunlp/TableLlama.

To comprehensively compare the performance of insight generation across multiple tables, we consider three types of baselines: proprietary LLMs, open-source LLMs, and SOTA table question-answering methods. Proprietary LLMs encompass commercial models renowned for their advanced reasoning and high-quality text generation capabilities. Open-source LLMs, serving as transparent and adaptable alternatives, leverage large-scale training data and advanced architectures to deliver performance that is competitive with proprietary counterparts. SOTA TQA methods are specialized approaches explicitly engineered for complex, table-aware reasoning tasks, prioritizing accuracy in tabular question-answering. We consider the following models as the baseline generators:

• o3-mini (OpenAI, 2025b) is a smaller yet advanced LLM developed by OpenAI, which is designed to efficiently solve complex problems by breaking them into constituent parts. In this study, we leverage o3-mini-2025-01-31 with reasoning effort parameter as medium.

• GPT-4o (OpenAI, 2024) is an advanced OpenAI’s proprietary LLM known for its enhanced reasoning capabilities and performance across various disciplines. In this study, we leverage gpt-4o-2024-08-06 checkpoint.

• Claude 3.5 Sonnet (Anthropic, ) is developed by Anthropic. It features improvements in coding proficiency and multimodal capabilities. In this study, we leverage claude-3-5-sonnet -20241022 checkpoint.

o3-mini (OpenAI, 2025b) is a smaller yet advanced LLM developed by OpenAI, which is designed to efficiently solve complex problems by breaking them into constituent parts. In this study, we leverage o3-mini-2025-01-31 with reasoning effort parameter as medium.

GPT-4o (OpenAI, 2024) is an advanced OpenAI’s proprietary LLM known for its enhanced reasoning capabilities and performance across various disciplines. In this study, we leverage gpt-4o-2024-08-06 checkpoint.

Claude 3.5 Sonnet (Anthropic, ) is developed by Anthropic. It features improvements in coding proficiency and multimodal capabilities. In this study, we leverage claude-3-5-sonnet -20241022 checkpoint.

• DeepSeek-R1-8B (DeepSeek-AI, 2025) is distilled version of DeepSeek-R1, which is a open-source LLM released by DeepSeek AI. In this study, we utilize unsloth/DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama -8B, which is based on Llama-3.1-8B.
• Qwen2-7B (Yang et al., 2024) is an open-source LLM developed by Alibaba Cloud, with the largest model containing 72 billion parameters. In this study, we utilize Qwen/Qwen2-7B- Instruct.

• Gemma-7B (Mesnard et al., 2024) is an open-source LLM developed by Google DeepMind, which is known for its multilingual capabilities and creative outputs. In this study, we utilize google/gemma-7b-it.

• Llama 3.1-8B (MetaAI, 2024) is an open-source LLM released by Meta AI, which is available in multiple sizes up to 405 billion parameters. In this study, we utilize meta-llama/Llama- 3.1-8B-Instruct.
• Mistral-7B (Jiang et al., 2023) is an open-source LLM developed by Mistral AI. In this study, we utilize mistralai/Mistral-7B- Instruct-v0.3.

DeepSeek-R1-8B (DeepSeek-AI, 2025) is distilled version of DeepSeek-R1, which is a open-source LLM released by DeepSeek AI. In this study, we utilize unsloth/DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama -8B, which is based on Llama-3.1-8B.

Gemma-7B (Mesnard et al., 2024) is an open-source LLM developed by Google DeepMind, which is known for its multilingual capabilities and creative outputs. In this study, we utilize google/gemma-7b-it.
Llama 3.1-8B (MetaAI, 2024) is an open-source LLM released by Meta AI, which is available in multiple sizes up to 405 billion parameters. In this study, we utilize meta-llama/Llama- 3.1-8B-Instruct.
Mistral-7B (Jiang et al., 2023) is an open-source LLM developed by Mistral AI. In this study, we utilize mistralai/Mistral-7B- Instruct-v0.3.
• Chain-of-Table (Wang et al., 2024b) is LLM-based method designed to enhance table-based reasoning. It employs iterative reconstruction of input table through dynamic tabular operations.

• TaPERA (Zhao et al., 2024) a modular framework designed to enhance faithfulness and interpretability in long-form table question answering by combining a QA-based content planner and execution-based reasoning..
• Dater (Ye et al., 2023) focuses on selecting relevant information from input tables and providing contextual information to support the statement verification process.

TaPERA (Zhao et al., 2024) a modular framework designed to enhance faithfulness and interpretability in long-form table question answering by combining a QA-based content planner and execution-based reasoning..

Following recent studies that utilize language models for table-related tasks (Chen, 2023; Seo et al., 2024), we serialize the table input into a flattened sequence to effectively represent table data for language model processing. The table title is enclosed within [TITLE] tags, followed by the table headers marked with [HEADER], where individual column names are separated by a vertical bar (|). Each row is prefixed with a [ROW] tag and an index, while cell values are separated by a vertical bar. This approach ensures that the table format is preserved while making the input compatible with language models. For example, the input table is formatted as follows:
[TITLE] title [HEADER] col 1 | col2 | ... [ROW 1] cell 1,1 | cell 1,2 | ... [ROW 2] cell 2,1 | ...

For the table-specific retriever, we fine-tune each model on a single-table QA dataset using the AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of for 5 epochs. To efficiently finetune the model, we adopt LoRA and set the parameters as rr = 8, α\alpha = 32. We use a constant learning rate schedule set at 2e-5, and train with the batch size of 1 on 4 NVIDIA A100 GPU. The inference of open-source LLMs are conducted using vLLM framework (Kwon et al., 2023). We set temperature to 0.0 for efficient and robust output generation.

To assess whether MT-RAIG Eval produces consistent results across different backbone LLMs, we analyze two open-source models (DeepSeek-R1-8B and Llama-3.1-8B) against the GPT-4o-mini, which is the original backbone of MT-RAIG Eval. Specifically, we measure how closely the evaluation scores from each open-source model align with those from GPT-4o-mini by calculating pairwise correlation. From the results in Table 8, we can observe that even when replacing the backbone with open-source models, the high correlation persists, demonstrating that our evaluation method is both reproducible and robust.

From the results in Table 6, we observe that proprietary models with larger model sizes generally show higher performance compared to open-source models that have relatively fewer parameters. To further investigate this, we conduct additional experiments by differ the parameter sizes of open-source LLM to understand the effect of model size in the table reasoning performance. Specifically, we evaluate DeepSeek-R1, which is most powerful open-source LLM among our baselines ranging from 8B to 70B parameters. From the results in Table 9, we confirm a general trend where increasing model size correlates with improved performance, with a particularly notable improvement in the faithfulness score. This discrepancy suggests that the model’s capacity to accurately interpret and reason about table structures (which is a prerequisite for faithfulness) is more directly enhanced by parameter scaling, compared to the broader task coverage implied by completeness.

To supplement Table 5, we present the Spearman correlation results alongside the Pearson correlation, in terms of faithfulness and completeness, as shown in Table 17. We report the full multi-table retrieval results in Table 19, MT-RAIG Eval Completeness score results of diverse baselines in Table 20, with respect to Precision, Recall, and F1 score across different top-k values. Additionally, we present insight generation results along with existing automatic metrics in Table 21.

We select representative examples of (1) MT-RAIG Bench in Table 22 and 23, (2) discarded data in Table 24 and 25, (3) MT-RAIG Eval Faithfulness and (4) Completeness evaluation in Table 26 and 27, respectively. Moreover, we present (5) comparison with MT-RAIG Eval results for each baseline in Table 28 and 29. Ensure that we display only the first three rows of each table to improve readability.
